summary: Updated: Bitcoin Portfolio Allocation — the crash view now shows how long you're underwater. A shaded band and a dashed pre-crash-level line mark the years the portfolio sits below where it stood when the crash hit, labelled with the number of years (or "not recovered within your horizon"). A compact year-by-year results line sits under the composition bars, the chart's vertical scale now stays fixed while you drag the crash year or switch recovery shapes (so scenarios are directly comparable), and you can deep-link straight into a configured crash view. Underwater is measured on the whole portfolio, not the bitcoin sleeve; none of it is a forecast.

Updated: Bitcoin Portfolio Allocation — drop a crash onto the year-by-year chart. A new "What if a crash lands along the way?" control lets you place a bitcoin crash on any year and pick its recovery (Fast, Historical, or Weak), then watch the value dip and recover against a no-bitcoin benchmark. The verdict says plainly whether the allocation ends ahead, behind, or roughly even by your horizon — and behind is a real answer for an early crash with a weak recovery at a short horizon. With annual rebalancing also on, you can see the reset buy bitcoin while it's down. The crash hits the bitcoin sleeve only, recovery shapes follow historical patterns, and none of it is a forecast.

Updated: Bitcoin Portfolio Allocation — a new "Your portfolio, year by year" chart. Watch a small bitcoin position drift from a sliver into a dominant share of the portfolio over your horizon, with a dashed crash line that shows how the same-depth bear takes a bigger bite as the position grows. Composition bars read your share today and at the horizon, and an optional comparison asks what annual rebalancing would do — with an honest note that on a smooth trend path rebalancing always shows the lower endpoint, so this is the drift trade-off, not a verdict on rebalancing. The year-by-year path is in the audit table and CSV.

New: Bitcoin Portfolio Allocation. How Much Bitcoin finds the optimal fraction; this answers a plainer question: over a long horizon, does adding bitcoin make your portfolio meaningfully better, despite the drawdowns? Set an allocation and get the answer with its cost in the same breath: the extra return, the drawdown you must be able to hold through, and how much of your portfolio's movement bitcoin drives. That movement is the engine of the return, not a hazard: volatility is not risk, and the real danger is being shaken out and selling into the fall. Bitcoin's growth is projected from its Power Law with a regime-aware default, and a transparency line explains why the number is what it is. The assumptions sit above the slider with a reset, and it makes no recommendation on what to pick.

New: The Bitcoin Retirement Stress Test. Take your retirement plan and drop a bear market into it, then change the one thing that decides the outcome: the year you retire. The same stack and spending can pass or fail on retirement year alone, because retiring early means selling into the crash before the stack has compounded. Compare early against late side by side, see whether the plan survives or runs dry, and read the levers that matter most: when you retire and how much you draw relative to your stack. It shows failure plainly and makes no predictions.

New: Bitcoin Bull & Bear Cycles — Bitcoin's best returns and deepest drawdowns are one fact, not two. A live read on where the current cycle sits, the four historical bears with a day-zero overlay, a computed volatility trend, why volatility isn't the same as losing your money, and why sizing is what lets you survive the fall. It refuses, on purpose, to guess where the bottom is.

Updated: The Bitcoin Retirement — major enhancements. A year-by-year view of your retirement (portfolio value, annual change, and growth), a "Verify the math" audit table where every number reconciles by hand and exports to CSV, and controls to explore the assumptions honestly: reverts-to-trend vs. today's gap-to-trend, nominal vs. real (today's) dollars, and whether your income target is in today's dollars or a fixed future amount. Escape-velocity and depletion now track whichever assumptions you pick.

New: Wait, or Deploy Now? — from any position in the channel, see whether waiting for a lower Bitcoin entry historically got you more coins, or whether the dip you'd be waiting for usually never came. The position-aware close to the deployment trilogy.

New: Your Bitcoin Deployment Plan — the personal companion to Lump Sum or Ladder In? Model your own sum as a lump, a ladder, or a hybrid, and see how it would have played out across real history and how it might play out ahead, shown as a range. With rising-channel cautions front and centre.

New: Lump Sum or Ladder In? — deploy a windfall all at once or spread it out, and watch the answer flip with where Bitcoin sits in the Power Law channel. Drag the scrubber: decisive when cheap, patient when stretched, committed always. A retrospective teaching demonstration of how the two compared.

New: Bitcoin & Metcalfe's Law — does price really grow with the square of adoption? Measured from on-chain data the exponent comes in below the quadratic ideal, and the deeper finding is that the signal itself is fading: as ownership moves into ETFs and custody, on-chain holder counts stop tracking who really owns Bitcoin. Interactive proxy/era selector with a live fit chart.

New: The Bitcoin Doubling Ladder — the Power Law seen through price doublings, with an interactive ladder, a deviation wave showing how far the market strays from trend, and a scenario slider for the rungs ahead.

New: Risks to Bitcoin — ten risks to the network, each stated in its strongest form and answered, from state attack and the security budget to quantum computing and a rival coin. Each closes with the markers that would tell us the risk is becoming real.
New: How Much Bitcoin? — the Kelly criterion's startling answer to position sizing, an interactive growth curve under Power Law scenarios, and the honest case for taking a fraction of the formula.

New: Paper Bitcoin vs. Real Bitcoin — an ETF, an exchange balance, a multisig vault, a hardware wallet: each is a different asset wearing bitcoin's price. An interactive property grid compares five ways of holding bitcoin across ten dimensions, from confiscation reach to inheritance.

New: Bitcoin and Fixed Income — how bitcoin-backed preferreds (STRC, SATA) compare against conventional fixed income and against simply holding bitcoin, across growth scenarios, stress overlays, and horizons. Five tabs from the structural question to the bear case in detail.

New: Bitcoin vs. Rental Property — the honest yield comparison after landlord costs and exit taxes. Includes an interactive calculator that runs your scenario across bitcoin growth and four exit paths. Now on the homepage and as carousel slide #3.

New: Start Here — a curated orientation pathway for newcomers. Seven explorations sequenced so each one earns the next (framework → emotion → quantification → introduction → mechanism → evidence → commitment), with explicit payoff per step and an applied-next-steps block at the end. Linked prominently from the homepage hero for 'New to Bitcoin?' visitors

The Gallery is now featured on the homepage's Latest section — a curated vertical scroll-through of the ten most striking charts across the site, each chart full-size with editorial framing. Designed as the highest-level entry point for any skeptical reader who wants the structural argument at a glance
The Gallery now has its own social card and search-engine baseline — links unfurl cleanly when shared, and it shows up in search alongside the other pages.

Small fix on The Bitcoin Horizon: the §2 chart caption (under the rolling-CAGR chart) was constrained to 72 characters wide and stopped well short of the chart's right edge. Removed the max-width — caption now flows to the full chart-panel width, matching the chart canvas above it

Gallery: the Volatility Is Not Risk chart now starts from 2015 — a more conservative window that still shows bitcoin's worst 10-year CAGR sitting comfortably above the S&P 500's. Plus a subtle 'you are here' marker on the Power Law chart.
Gallery: the Heatmap chart gains a 'Held to today' toggle — most of the red losses turn amber once you keep holding.
Gallery v2 complete. Final three charts live: the BTC vs S&P 500 outperformance grid, the half-life of a dollar's purchasing power, and how bitcoin's CAGR variance compresses over longer horizons.

Gallery Chart 7 rebuilt: replaced the $10K-invested-in-2010 wealth chart (a cherry-pick of the best possible start year) with a rolling 4-year CAGR comparison. Bitcoin's returns decline over time but still beat the S&P 500 and NASDAQ-100 in every window.
Gallery polish: clearer section dividers between charts, a sharper Chart 2 title, and more concrete claims in the Power Law vs S&P 500 convergence footnote.

Gallery Commit C: three more charts live. Global housing affordability across HK / Sydney / Vancouver / London / US (Demographia ratios), BTC needed for retirement decay curve (4% rule, anchors at year 0/+10/+20/+30), and $10K invested in 2010 — bitcoin vs S&P 500 TR vs NASDAQ-100 TR. Seven charts done; three to go in the final commit

Gallery Commit B: three more charts live. Power Law implied CAGR vs. S&P 500 (with convergence footnote), BTC required to buy the median US house (2013→2025 actual, projected trend to 2032), and the real opportunity cost of buying a house (start-year toggle 2014/2016/2018/2020/2022). Four charts done; six to go

Gallery Chart 1 polish: white historical line now extends to today's live price; hover tooltip shows trend + floor + upper at the hover X (not just nearest); today marker has the radial-gradient halo from the Disciplined Rebalancing chart; editorial prose spans the full chart width for a tighter scroll rhythm between charts

The Gallery rebuilt as a vertical scroll-through of full charts (was a teaser card grid). Chart 1 of 10 live: the Power Law channel in ±2y default view with All-time / Near / Planning toggle. Nine more charts arriving in three follow-up commits
Gallery palette finally rendering correctly (real fix: nested <style> tags in page CSS were breaking the :root variable block). Calculators featured tiles now have matched heights
Calculators page: featured row now leads with The Bitcoin Retirement (with its live mini-preview) — Heatmap moved out, since it's not strictly a calculator and already lives in The Gallery
Gallery polish: page now renders against the proper dark palette (body background + CSS variables were missing on first ship). Calculators page H1 updated to match the nav label
The Gallery is open: 8 chart cards, with the Power Law channel as a live hero and the rest as v1 hand-drawn previews — a visual table-of-contents for the site
Homepage ticker polish: ₿ + price in Bitcoin orange with a slow heartbeat glow
New section: The Gallery — a visual index of charts across the site (placeholder for now; first chart cards land shortly). Nav: Tools renamed to Calculators
Bitcoin Retirement calculator: wider input sliders (3-column grid) now that withdrawal rate is a derived readout, allowing finer-grained dragging
Bitcoin Retirement calculator: withdrawal rate now a derived readout under target income, making the mathematical linkage between the two visible
Bitcoin vs. Real Estate calculator: Power Law scenarios clarified (Upper = cycle peak, ~3× trend) and canonical ? tooltips throughout
Bitcoin vs. Real Estate calculator: Real / Nominal display toggle for projected values, with inflation-aware tooltips
Bitcoin vs. Real Estate calculator: input formatting polish ($/comma/%) and clearer projected-value phrasing with future-date context
Bitcoin vs. Real Estate calculator: cross-session persistence for user inputs + reformed NET headline on retrospective
Bitcoin vs. Real Estate: seesaw chart start-year selector + start-price transparency in comparison table
Bitcoin vs. Real Estate: new Global Affordability Crisis chart (5-market Demographia data, Tokyo case study)
Power Law pages: live BTC price + consistent today-anchor across Disciplined Rebalancing, Bitcoin Retirement, and Borrowing Against Your Stack
Homepage: new What Money Is For exploration added to carousel with silent video intro
